content.text: Every Python package on PyPI can publish new versions at any moment. Without locking, a Friday deploy of an agent service may install a tokenizer or SDK with subtly changed defaults — not because you changed any code, but because an upstream maintainer published a patch. Dependency pinning fixes exact package versions for every environment by committing a lockfile that records the fully-resolved dependency graph, including all transitive packages and their cryptographic hashes. For agentic systems whose correctness depends on specific model SDKs, tool-call serialisation, and retry behaviour, pinning is not optional.

Dependency pinning is the practice of recording exact package versions — and optionally their content hashes — for every package in a project's environment, including transitive (indirect) dependencies that your direct dependencies pull in. The record is stored in a
that sits beside the project's human-authored dependency specification.
The two-file model is key: an
) expresses loose, human-readable constraints such as
) is machine-generated and records the one exact solution the resolver chose:
plus every transitive package at its pinned version, with a sha256 hash per wheel.

The lockfile is committed to source control. Everyone who clones the repo — a developer, a CI runner, a production Docker build — installs from the lockfile, not from the loose spec, getting bit-for-bit identical packages. The lockfile is never hand-edited; it is regenerated by the tool whenever the intent file changes or a deliberate upgrade is requested.

# uv.lock excerpt (TOML format)
[[package]]
name = "openai"
version = "1.40.2"
source = { registry = "https://pypi.org/simple" }
sdist = { url = "...", hash = "sha256:a3c..." }

[[package]]
name = "httpx"
version = "0.27.2"
source = { registry = "https://pypi.org/simple" }
dependencies = [
  { name = "httpcore" },
  { name = "certifi" },
]
wheels = [
  { url = "...", hash = "sha256:b1e..." },
]

Agent systems are particularly sensitive to dependency drift because their outputs are probabilistic and their correctness depends on the exact behaviour of the SDK layer sitting between application code and the model API. A minor version bump to an LLM SDK might:
or backoff algorithm, altering how the agent behaves under API rate limits
Modify the JSON serialisation of tool calls, breaking downstream parsers that expect a specific field order
Introduce a new prompt-formatting helper that gets activated by a keyword in system prompts, shifting model outputs
Update a tokeniser dependency, causing token counts to differ and hitting context-window limits in different places
Quietly drop a deprecated parameter that your code still passes, replacing it with a default that behaves differently

These changes are often not breaking in the semver sense — the library's public API is unchanged — but they are breaking for an agent whose evaluation suite measures factual accuracy, tool-use success rate, or latency distributions. Without pinning, CI might pass on Monday (when the lockfile was last regenerated) and fail on Thursday on the same commit because CI ran
and picked up 1.40.7 published on Wednesday.

Pinning is also the contract between environments. Production must run the same dependency graph that staging tested. Without a lockfile, divergence is guaranteed over time as different machines resolve dependencies at different moments. A lockfile makes this contract explicit and machine-checkable.
— the same install regardless of when, where, or by whom it is run. A clone made two years later installs exactly the versions that passed evaluation today.
— version changes happen only through a deliberate lockfile-update commit, reviewed in a PR with an eval gate. No surprise upgrades between deploys.
— the lockfile diff in a PR shows exactly which packages changed, which makes root-cause analysis after regressions tractable.
— pinned versions map to specific CVE advisories. Tools like
can scan the lockfile and report known vulnerabilities.
— a stable lockfile produces a stable
layer that the Docker build cache can reuse across commits, dramatically reducing build times.
— rolling back a deploy means redeploying a previous image whose Dockerfile pinned the old lockfile. The git commit SHA of the lockfile is an exact identifier for the dependency state.
— when evaluating two agent configurations against each other, a shared lockfile eliminates the SDK version as a confound.
— pinned packages become stale. Security patches must be applied deliberately; they do not flow in automatically. Teams need an update cadence (often automated via Renovate or Dependabot).

— two branches that each add a dependency will produce conflicts in the lockfile. Modern lock formats (TOML-based uv.lock, poetry.lock) sort packages alphabetically and are designed to resolve cleanly, but conflicts still occur.

— wheels differ by OS and CPU architecture. A lockfile generated on macOS arm64 may specify different wheels than one generated on Linux x86_64. Tools like uv support universal locks with platform markers; pip-compile requires separate lock files per platform.

— published libraries (packages you release to PyPI) should NOT ship lockfiles. Lockfiles are for applications and services. A library's lockfile would conflict with downstream pinning. Libraries declare only loose constraints in
— for large dependency graphs, resolving and computing hashes can take 20–60 seconds. uv is substantially faster than pip-compile for this step.

Version resolution is a constraint-satisfaction problem. The resolver takes a set of version constraints from the intent file, queries package metadata from the index, builds a directed dependency graph, and finds an assignment of exact versions that satisfies every constraint simultaneously — including constraints introduced by transitive dependencies.
For example, if your intent file says
. It must find a version of httpx that satisfies both
, is recorded in the lockfile along with every other transitive package.
After selecting versions, the tool downloads each wheel and computes its sha256 hash, which is also recorded. This hash is the basis for
verification: when installing from the lock, pip checks the downloaded wheel's hash against the recorded value, detecting corrupted downloads or man-in-the-middle substitutions.

The intent file is the source of truth for human decisions: which packages the project needs and what version ranges are acceptable. The lockfile is the source of truth for machines: the single exact solution the resolver found. This split is important because it lets you express upgrade flexibility (
accepts any compatible release) while still ensuring every environment uses one specific version.
flag that installs from the lockfile without re-resolving. If

and the lockfile are out of sync (e.g., you added a package to the intent file without regenerating the lock), the install fails loudly. This is the correct behaviour for CI and production: never silently upgrade.

during install enforces cryptographic integrity. uv and poetry include hashes automatically. This prevents a tampered package from slipping into a production deployment even if the version number matches.

The diagram below shows the complete lockfile lifecycle, from the human-authored intent file through the resolver to the lockfile, which then fans out to identical installs on developer machines, CI, and production. Automated tools (Renovate) feed back into the resolver to propose controlled upgrades.

attrs.alt: Dependency pinning and lockfile workflow: intent file → resolver → lockfile → identical installs on dev, CI, and production

content.text: The key architectural property is that the lockfile is the single source of truth for installed packages. All three consumers (developer machine, CI pipeline, production Docker image) install from the same lockfile commit. The only way packages change is through a lockfile-update commit, which must pass the eval gate before merging.
Use lockfiles in every application or service — especially agentic systems. The decision tree is straightforward:
: production agent services, CI eval pipelines, staging environments, Docker images, any deployable artefact
: services that integrate with external model APIs, where SDK behaviour affects agent outputs
Lock on main, relax on feature branches
: it is acceptable to work without regenerating the lock during early exploration, but merging to
Do NOT ship a lockfile in published libraries
only; let the downstream application own the lock
themselves are not typically locked — they are bootstrapped separately
Also pin the Python runtime itself. A Docker image should specify
(which floats to the latest patch). The runtime version affects standard-library behaviour, typing semantics, and asyncio internals — all of which matter for agents.
Pick one tool and use it consistently across the team. The three main options for Python agent projects are:
lockfile; works well in Dockerfile-centric workflows
; heavier but convenient for projects that also publish

# pyproject.toml — direct dependencies only, loose constraints
[project]
name = "my-agent"
requires-python = ">=3.12"
dependencies = [
    "openai>=1.40,<2",
    "httpx>=0.27",
    "fastapi>=0.110,<0.130",
    "pydantic>=2.6",
]

[dependency-groups]
dev = [
    "pytest>=8.0",
    "pip-audit>=2.7",
]

# uv (recommended)
uv lock                         # generates uv.lock
uv sync                         # install from lock into venv

# pip-tools
pip-compile pyproject.toml -o requirements.lock --generate-hashes
pip-sync requirements.lock

# poetry
poetry lock                     # generates poetry.lock
poetry install --only main

# The lockfile belongs in source control — always.
# Never add it to .gitignore.
git add uv.lock                 # or requirements.lock / poetry.lock
git commit -m "chore: pin dependencies"

# Verify the lock is not in .gitignore accidentally
grep -r "\.lock" .gitignore     # should return nothing (or only *.lock lines you intend)
Step 5 — Enforce frozen installs in CI and Docker

# Dockerfile — frozen install, no re-resolution at build time
FROM python:3.12.4-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Copy lockfile and intent file first (layer cache)
COPY pyproject.toml uv.lock ./

# Install uv, then sync frozen
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir uv==0.4.15 && \
    uv sync --frozen --no-dev

COPY . .
CMD ["python", "-m", "my_agent"]

# .github/workflows/ci.yml
- name: Install dependencies
  run: |
    pip install uv==0.4.15
    uv sync --frozen            # fails if uv.lock is out of date with pyproject.toml
Example 1 — Generating a lockfile with uv (introductory)
This example walks through the minimal setup for a new agent project using uv.

# 1. Create a project directory
mkdir my-agent && cd my-agent

# 2. Write the intent file
cat > pyproject.toml << 'EOF'
[project]
name = "my-agent"
requires-python = ">=3.12"
dependencies = [
    "openai>=1.40,<2",
    "pydantic>=2.6",
]
EOF

# 3. Generate the lockfile
uv lock
# → Resolves 12 packages, writes uv.lock

# 4. Install (creates .venv/)
uv sync

# 5. Verify the exact versions installed
uv pip freeze
# openai==1.40.2
# httpx==0.27.2
# pydantic==2.8.1
# ...

# 6. Confirm lock is current
uv lock --check       # exits 0 if lock matches pyproject.toml, non-zero if stale
This example shows the pip-tools workflow used in CI/CD pipelines where hash verification provides supply-chain security.

# requirements.in — direct dependencies, kept in version control
cat requirements.in
#   openai>=1.40,<2
#   httpx>=0.27
#   pydantic>=2.6
#   fastapi>=0.110,<0.130
#   uvicorn>=0.29

# 1. Compile (resolve + generate hashes)
pip-compile requirements.in \
  --output-file requirements.lock \
  --generate-hashes \
  --no-header \
  --no-annotate

# 2. Inspect the result
head -20 requirements.lock
# annotated-types==0.7.0 \
#     --hash=sha256:04e02d... \
#     --hash=sha256:1468a2...
# certifi==2024.7.4 \
#     --hash=sha256:c198a5...
# openai==1.40.2 \
#     --hash=sha256:a3c9e7... \
#     --hash=sha256:f1d4b2...

# 3. Install with hash enforcement
pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock

# 4. In CI, add a check that the lock is current:
pip-compile requirements.in --dry-run 2>&1 | grep "Would pin" && {
    echo "ERROR: requirements.lock is stale. Run pip-compile and commit."
    exit 1
}

# 5. Scan for CVEs
pip-audit --requirement requirements.lock --format json | \
  python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); sys.exit(1 if d['vulnerabilities'] else 0)"
Example 3 — Python script: verify lockfile freshness
This script can run in CI to confirm the lockfile reflects the current intent file, catching the common mistake of forgetting to regenerate the lock after editing

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
ci_check_lock.py
Verify that uv.lock is up to date with pyproject.toml.
Exits 1 (with explanation) if stale; exits 0 if current.
"""
import subprocess
import sys

def check_uv_lock_current() -> bool:
    """Return True if uv.lock matches pyproject.toml exactly."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["uv", "lock", "--check"],
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    return result.returncode == 0

def check_hashes_match(lock_path: str = "requirements.lock") -> bool:
    """Return True if every hash in requirements.lock can be verified locally."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["pip", "install", "--dry-run", "--require-hashes", "-r", lock_path],
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    return result.returncode == 0

def main() -> None:
    print("Checking lockfile freshness...")
    if not check_uv_lock_current():
        print(
            "ERROR: uv.lock is stale.\n"
            "  Run: uv lock\n"
            "  Then commit the updated uv.lock."
        )
        sys.exit(1)
    print("uv.lock is current.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Consider a production agent service that processes customer support tickets using tool calls. The service runs on Kubernetes with 10 replicas, deploys multiple times per week via GitOps, and maintains a 500-case golden eval suite that gates all merges.
The dependency management setup looks like this:
Renovate opens a PR every Monday bumping patch versions. The PR title includes the lockfile diff summary (e.g.,
(fails immediately if the lock is stale), then executes the 500-case eval suite. A >2% drop in tool-use success rate blocks the merge.
, so the package install layer is cached across commits that touch only application code.
Each deployed image is labelled with
(the git SHA of the last lockfile change), making it easy to correlate a production issue with the exact dependency state.

# Production Dockerfile (excerpt)
FROM python:3.12.4-slim AS base

ARG LOCK_SHA
LABEL lock_sha=${LOCK_SHA}

WORKDIR /app

# Layer 1: install uv (rarely changes)
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir uv==0.4.15

# Layer 2: install deps from locked graph (cached until lock changes)
COPY pyproject.toml uv.lock ./
RUN uv sync --frozen --no-dev --no-cache

# Layer 3: application code (changes frequently)
COPY src/ ./src/

CMD ["python", "-m", "agent.server"]
When an incident occurs and the on-call engineer suspects a dependency as the cause, they compare the lock SHA of the running image against the previous deployment's SHA using
. If the lockfile changed in the last deploy, a one-line rollback command redeploys the previous image, immediately restoring the previous dependency state.

The most damaging mistake is gitignoring the lockfile. Teams sometimes treat it like a build artefact, but it is source. Without the lockfile in source control, every environment resolves independently, defeating the entire purpose of pinning.

# WRONG — do not do this
echo "*.lock" >> .gitignore
echo "requirements.lock" >> .gitignore

# CORRECT — commit the lockfile
git add uv.lock
git status  # should show uv.lock as tracked
without pip-compile pins one package but leaves all transitive dependencies floating. The lock must cover the full resolved tree.

# WRONG — partial pinning, transitive deps still float
# requirements.txt (hand-written)
openai==1.40.2
fastapi==0.115.0
# httpx, anyio, pydantic-core, etc. are NOT pinned

# CORRECT — use pip-compile or uv lock
# requirements.lock (pip-compile output, 47 packages pinned)
anyio==4.4.0
certifi==2024.7.4
fastapi==0.115.0
httpcore==1.0.5
httpx==0.27.2
openai==1.40.2
pydantic==2.8.1
pydantic-core==2.20.1
sniffio==1.3.1
# ... 38 more
in CI allows the resolver to update the lock silently if the intent file has changed. This means CI can succeed with a different dependency set than the committed lock.

# WRONG — may silently re-resolve
uv sync

# CORRECT — fails loudly if lock is stale
uv sync --frozen
Mistake 4 — Upgrading across major SDK versions without eval

across a major is never a patch. The API surface, response shapes, and exception hierarchy change. Merging such a bump without running the eval suite risks silent regressions in tool-call parsing, streaming behaviour, or error handling.
Mistake 5 — Using pip freeze to create a lock

# WRONG — captures everything installed in the active venv,
# including accidental dev tools and unrelated packages
pip freeze > requirements.txt

# CORRECT — compile from a curated intent file
pip-compile requirements.in -o requirements.lock --generate-hashes
— treat it as source code, not a build artefact. Codify this with a CI check that fails if the lock is not tracked.
Use --frozen everywhere that is not a developer's local first-install
— CI, Docker build, and any scripted environment must install frozen.
Separate the intent file from the lock
) as the human-authored source of intent. Never put exact versions in the intent file; put them only in the lock.
— configure Renovate or Dependabot to open weekly PRs that bump patch versions. Review the lockfile diff and eval results rather than the code diff.
Require manual review for minor/major SDK bumps
— automate patches; gate minor and major bumps on a dedicated PR with a full eval run and a manual signoff.
Scan the lockfile for CVEs in CI
catches known vulnerabilities before they reach production.
Label deployed images with the lockfile SHA
in the Dockerfile makes rollback and blame straightforward.
Pin the Python interpreter version too
in Docker. The runtime is as important as the packages.
so production installs exclude test frameworks, linters, and dev tools.

Lockfile-based installs are faster than unlocked installs at scale because the resolver does not run. Once the lock exists, every consumer just fetches the specified wheels and verifies hashes — a deterministic, parallelisable download operation.

The most impactful performance technique for containerised agents is structuring the Dockerfile to maximise layer cache reuse. Copy the lockfile before application code, so the package install layer is invalidated only when the lock changes, not on every code commit:

# Optimal layer order
COPY pyproject.toml uv.lock ./      # invalidated only when deps change
RUN uv sync --frozen --no-dev       # cache hit on 95%+ of commits

COPY src/ ./src/                    # invalidated on every code change
For large graphs (50+ packages), resolver time dominates the initial lock generation. Rough benchmarks on a graph of 80 packages:

These figures are for illustration; actual times depend on network speed and graph size. The key takeaway is that uv's resolver is one to two orders of magnitude faster than pip-based tools, making it preferable for large projects or frequent CI runs.
Parallel installs and caching in CI
CI platforms cache the virtual environment or the uv/pip download cache between runs. Cache the directory that holds downloaded wheels (uv:
) keyed on the lockfile hash. A cache miss re-downloads; a cache hit completes in seconds.

# GitHub Actions: cache uv downloads keyed on lockfile hash
- uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: ~/.cache/uv
    key: uv-${{ hashFiles('uv.lock') }}
    restore-keys: uv-

- run: uv sync --frozen
uv is the fastest Python package and project manager available as of 2025. Its
in a structured TOML format that is readable in diffs.
installs exactly what the lock specifies. uv supports workspaces (monorepo), universal locks with platform markers, and direct integration with

# uv essential commands
uv lock                   # generate/update uv.lock from pyproject.toml
uv lock --check           # verify lock is current (CI gate)
uv sync --frozen          # install from lock (no resolution)
uv sync --frozen --no-dev # production install (skip dev group)
uv add httpx              # add dependency, update lock
uv remove httpx           # remove dependency, update lock
uv lock --upgrade-package openai  # upgrade one package, keep others
(install from lock, removing unlisted packages). It is the most portable option, producing plain-text files that work with standard

# pip-tools workflow
pip install pip-tools

# Compile with hashes
pip-compile pyproject.toml \
  --output-file requirements.lock \
  --generate-hashes \
  --strip-extras

# Install frozen
pip-sync requirements.lock

# Upgrade a specific package
pip-compile --upgrade-package openai pyproject.toml -o requirements.lock
poetry manages dependencies, virtual environments, and publishing in one tool. Its
is a structured TOML file. It is well-suited to projects that also publish Python packages.

poetry lock             # generate/update poetry.lock
poetry install          # install all groups
poetry install --only main   # production install
poetry add openai@^1.40      # add, update lock
poetry update openai         # upgrade, update lock

Automated dependency update bots open PRs that bump lockfile versions on a schedule. Configure them to update patch versions weekly and open separate PRs for minor/major bumps with a longer review window:

# pip-audit — scans against PyPI Advisory Database
pip install pip-audit
pip-audit --requirement requirements.lock

# osv-scanner — scans against OSV database (broader)
osv-scanner --lockfile requirements.lock
osv-scanner --lockfile uv.lock
osv-scanner --lockfile poetry.lock   # supports multiple formats

# In CI, fail the build on any vulnerability:
pip-audit --requirement requirements.lock --fail-on-cvss 7.0
The table below compares the main approaches to managing dependencies in a Python agent project.

Lockfiles are the right tool for the vast majority of agent projects. Vendoring is occasionally appropriate in air-gapped environments. Container image pinning (using a content-addressed image digest) complements but does not replace lockfiles — the image may have been built with a stale lock.

Dependency pinning with hash verification provides significant protection against supply-chain attacks. If an attacker publishes a malicious version of a package under the same version number (dependency confusion attack) or modifies a wheel after its initial publication, the hash check fails and the install aborts.

# Install with hash enforcement (pip-tools workflow)
pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
# → ERROR: THESE PACKAGES DO NOT MATCH THE HASHES FROM requirements.lock:
#     malicious-package==1.2.3 from https://pypi.org/...

# uv includes hash verification by default
uv sync --frozen  # verifies all hashes automatically

Pin your private packages by name and ensure your pip/uv configuration prioritises your private index. Without pinning, a public package with a name similar to your private one can be installed accidentally.

# pip.ini or uv.toml — prioritise private index
[global]
index-url = https://your-private-pypi.example.com/simple
extra-index-url = https://pypi.org/simple

# Better: use index priority (uv supports this natively)
# uv.toml
[pip]
index = "https://your-private-pypi.example.com/simple"
extra-index = ["https://pypi.org/simple"]
When a CVE is published for a pinned package, the standard response is:
Renovate or a security bot opens a PR with the patched version
to confirm the CVE is resolved
CI runs the eval suite to confirm no regression
PR is merged under emergency review; deployment follows the normal rollout path
Pinning does not slow CVE response — it makes the response traceable and testable. Without pinning, a "quick fix"
on a running server is invisible in the audit trail and cannot be rolled back cleanly.
Reliability: preventing version drift in long-running services

Container orchestrators restart pods on crash; if the restart pulls a new package version (because the image was not content-addressed and the install was not frozen), the restarted pod may have a different dependency graph than the pod it replaced. Lockfiles eliminate this class of reliability incident entirely: every restart installs from the same locked graph that the image was built and tested with.
A lockfile records the exact versions and sha256 hashes of every package in the full dependency graph — not just direct deps — and is the single source of truth for all installs.

Agentic systems are particularly vulnerable to unlocked dependency drift because SDK minor versions can change tool-call serialisation, retry behaviour, and tokeniser output without triggering a semver major bump.
Use the two-file model: a human-authored intent file with loose constraints, and a machine-generated lockfile with exact versions. Never hand-edit the lockfile.
in CI and Docker builds; a stale lock should fail loudly, not silently re-resolve to a different graph.
Commit the lockfile to source control unconditionally; treat it as source, not a build artefact.
Automate patch-level bumps (Renovate/Dependabot) and gate every bump on the eval suite; require manual review for minor and major SDK version changes.
Hash verification (enabled by default in uv; opt-in with
in pip-tools) protects against supply-chain attacks by rejecting any wheel whose content does not match the recorded hash.
Label every deployed image with the lockfile's git SHA to make rollback and incident attribution straightforward.
Pin the Python interpreter version alongside packages; the runtime is as consequential as any library for agent behaviour.
